title: Meet the Committee
text:
Meet the Committee
The R-HTA in LMICs team comprises individuals from around the world with diverse backgrounds, all united by a commitment to improving healthcare decision-making through open-source tools and collaborative learning.

Co-Chairs








Buhle Ndweni


Lecturer in Health Economics


Buhle is Co-chair and founding director of the ‘R-HTA in LMICs’ Chapter. She’s currently a PhD Candidate at the University of Cape Town, South Africa, where she’s part of an NIHR Global health Research funded clinical trial investigating fungal infections in patients living with advanced HIV disease. She holds an MSc Health Economics and Decision Modelling (MSc HEDM) degree from the University of Sheffield, UK. She is skilled in building economic models and performing data analysis using Excel, VBA, R, STATA, Simul8 and WinBUGS (for Bayesian analysis using Markov chain Monte Carlo methods). Ndweni is passionate about global health research and finding innovative solutions to improve access to affordable, quality healthcare for all, especially disadvantaged communities in LMICs. She is on a quest to make a significant contribution in healthcare development and investment across the African continent and beyond.

Show more 











Carina Behr


Senior Health Economist


Carina is a senior health economist at Dark Peak Analytics, building and reviewing cost-effectiveness models and bespoke Shiny apps for clients to streamline the process from clinical trial data, survival analysis, cost-effectiveness analysis and automated reporting. Carina completed her PhD in health economic modelling, at the University of Twente in the Netherlands, where her research focused on a multi-disease screening program which included building a microsimulation model in R. Before discovering health economicsduring her Masters, Carina obtained a BSc and an MSc in Industrial engineering from the University of Pretoria and University of Twente, respectively. Carina is a founding member of R-HTA for LMICS.

Show more 







Scientific Committee Members








Joshua Soboil


Health Economist


Joshua is a founding member of R-HTA in LMICs and former Co-Chair. Joshua enjoys problem solving and has developed a special love for evidence synthesis and advanced health economic decision-modelling techniques, using languages such as BUGS/JAGS and Stan coupled with R. His own work includes the ongoing development of a sensitivity analysis framework for HTA in Middle-Income Countries (MICs).

Show more 











Islem Meherzi


Data Science Engineer & R Consultant


Islem is a Senior Data Scientist with a background in Software Engineering and Business Intelligence. She has over 8 years of experience in data management, automation, and DevOps. She builds reliable systems that automate workflows, improve data quality, and support better decision-making. Her expertise includes R programming, data science, NLP, RPA, automated testing, DevOps, and Quarto. She has led projects spanning data engineering, analytics, and research-driven solutions. She has worked as a freelancer, Principal Data Scientist, and independent Data Specialist, bringing both technical depth and real-world impact to her work. Islem is also a researcher passionate about advancing healthcare through data. She enjoys writing, teaching, and sharing knowledge through reports, research papers, and technical content.

Show more 

 









Satoshi Koiso, MDP, BVSc


Research Specialist


Satoshi is a research specialist with expertise in health policy and decision analyses, focusing on simulation modeling and cost-effectiveness evaluations. He holds a Bachelor of Veterinary Science from the University of Tokyo and a Master of Development Practice from UC Berkeley. His research spans disease modeling, public health strategies, and global health policy, driven by a commitment to advancing evidence-based decision-making in resource-limited settings. As a Science Committee member of R-HTA in LMICs, Satoshi leverages his proficiency in R to support health technology assessment and foster capacity-building among researchers and practitioners in low- and middle-income countries.

Show more 











Hannibal Tafadzwa Musarurwa


Senior Lecturer


Hannibal is a distinguished senior academic at the Medical School of Walter Sisulu University in Mthatha. With a robust background in biotechnology, molecular biology, science communication, and biostatistics, he has significantly advanced research in traditional medicine as well as cardiovascular and metabolic physiology. Currently, his academic endeavours focus on the pivotal roles of traditional health practitioners in rural communities and the effects of antiretroviral drugs on the microbiome. Adopting a transdisciplinary and interdisciplinary approach, he seamlessly connects scientific research with community health, championing holistic and inclusive healthcare delivery in culturally and epidemiologically diverse low- and middle-income settings. Moreover, he plays an active role in scientific committees that empower scientists and researchers from these regions to utilise R for health technology assessments, while also serving as a judge for PAICTA hackathons.

Show more 











Gursimer Jeet, PhD


Public Health Researcher & Health Economist


Gursimer is a public health researcher and health economist with over a decade of experience in evaluating and synthesizing evidence for innovative public health and clinical interventions in maternal and child health, non-communicable diseases, and digital health. She has collaborated with policymakers, academics, and healthcare providers to generate policy-relevant insights. Proficient in multiple data analysis and evidence synthesis tools, she actively promotes the use of free, open-source software to support researchers with limited resources. She routinely uses R for data analysis, systematic reviews, and reproducible workflows, enabling transparent and impactful knowledge translation. Her work has informed peer-reviewed publications and capacity-building initiatives across low-, middle-, and high-income countries.

Show more

Past Events
A record of our previous workshops, webinars, and community events. All materials are shared openly after each session.




Introduction to R for HTA Modelling – 25 September 2023


Date: September 25, 2023


This half-day interactive tutorial trained LMIC health analysts on using R for HTA modelling. No prior R experience was required.

Participants learned how to convert Excel-based models to R and build interactive tools with Shiny.

title: R for HTA in LMICs
text:
Who We Are
R-HTA in LMICs is the regional chapter of the global R-HTA consortium. We promote the use of R for health technology assessment in LMICs through open-access tutorials, workshops, and curated resources.
Learn More About Us →

title: R for HTA in LMICs
text:
What We Do


Workshops & Training
Hands-on R training sessions for health economists — from introductory modelling to advanced cost-effectiveness analysis.

Browse Workshops »



Learning Resources
Curated tools, packages, and references for applying R in HTA contexts across diverse settings.

Explore Resources »



Events
Workshops and webinars led by international experts, open to students, academics, and practitioners.

View Past Events »

title: R for HTA in LMICs
text:
Get Involved
We welcome collaborators, learners, and partner institutions. There are several ways to connect with us:

Explore our open-source code and materials on GitHub
Contact us with questions, collaboration ideas, or to request support for your institution
Join our mailing list via the R-HTA main site

title: About R-HTA in LMICs
text:
R-HTA is a consortium dedicated to promoting the use of R for health economics and health technology assessment (HTA). The R for HTA in LMICs chapter is the regional arm of this consortium, focused on bringing open-source analytical capacity to HTA analysts and healthcare institutions in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). We host an annual workshop and quarterly tutorials, providing a platform where LMIC students and members of partner organisations can present their work, share experiences, and develop a wide range of R-based analytical skills.



To empower health systems in LMICs with cutting-edge, reproducible, and accessible analytical tools for HTA — built on the R programming language.
Learn more about our workshops and learning resources designed for both beginners and experienced practitioners.




While R-HTA operates as a global consortium, our LMIC chapter addresses challenges and opportunities that are specific to resource-constrained settings. We focus on:

Regional capacity building — partnering with universities, ministries of health, and research institutions across Africa, Asia, and Latin America to embed R-based HTA skills locally
Accessible, context-relevant training — developing tutorials and workshop content grounded in LMIC datasets, disease burdens, and health system realities
Reducing software cost barriers — promoting R as a free, powerful alternative to costly proprietary tools that many LMIC institutions cannot afford
Community of practice — connecting analysts across LMICs so that knowledge, code, and experience can be shared across borders

Contact us to discuss how we can support your institution or region.




HTA in LMICs faces distinct challenges:

Heavy reliance on proprietary software such as Microsoft Excel and TreeAge
Limited institutional budgets and restricted access to advanced analytical tools
A shortage of hands-on, practical R training tailored to HTA contexts

At the same time, health systems in LMICs are rapidly evolving — particularly under the drive toward Universal Health Coverage (UHC) — creating an urgent need for:

Complex modelling approaches (e.g. oncology, rare diseases, infectious disease)
Scalable, transparent, and reproducible analytical frameworks
Local capacity-building and sustainable knowledge transfer






Workshops & Training — covering R fundamentals, HTA methodology, essential R packages, and practical modelling techniques across diverse contexts. See the Workshops page for details.
Advanced tutorials — building cost-effectiveness models and interactive decision tools using Shiny.
Free resources and reproducible code — all available on our GitHub.

We work alongside partners to provide mentoring, technical assistance, and bespoke solutions for regional health needs.




Scalable
R handles complex, multi-state HTA models more efficiently than spreadsheet-based tools. Read more
Reproducible
With Git, Quarto/RMarkdown, and Shiny, models can be shared and version-controlled, easily updated and audited, and delivered as interactive, shareable applications.
Open and Community-Driven
R’s vibrant health economics ecosystem includes widely used packages such as BCEA (Bayesian cost-effectiveness analysis), heemod (Markov models for HTA), hesim (health economic simulation), and dampack (decision analytic modelling).




We welcome collaborators, learners, and institutions alike — join us in building a stronger foundation for HTA in LMICs using R.
Visit our contact page to connect with the team.

title: Workshops
text:
The R-HTA in LMICs chapter hosts annual workshops showcasing R applications in health economics, featuring contributions from students, academics, and practitioners across LMICs. All materials are shared openly after each event.

title: Learning Resources
text:
Learning Resources
A curated collection of tools, packages, tutorials, and references to support R-based health technology assessment across a range of experience levels and contexts.



Books & References


Recommended books for HTA using R



R for Health Technology Assessment: A comprehensive guide to the end-to-end use of R in health technology assessment (HTA), covering survival analysis, decision trees, multistate models, network meta-analysis, and discrete event simulation — all explained through real-world examples with annotated R code. Ideal for HTA modellers, regulators, and health economists, enriched with case studies contributed by community members.

Authors: Gianluca Baio, Howard Thom, Petros Pechlivanoglou


Community Contributors: Buhle Ndweni, Joshua Soboil, Federico Cairoli






Basic R


Introduction to R programming and scripting



Intro2R script: Input/output and object manipulation


Intro tutorial materials: Hands-on R learning materials from the June workshop





Intermediate Modelling


Modeling tools based on DARTH group’s materials



Time-Dependent Models paper (2022): Introduction to time-dependent decision models


Simulation script: R script for simulating time-dependent models


State-residence time model: Simulating state-residence times


Functions for analysis: R functions used for HTA model simulations





Advanced & Interactive Modelling


Advanced models and Shiny apps



Advanced HIV Markov model in R: Full workflow of a health economic Markov model


Explore interactive app: Interactive HIV model using Shiny





Additional Resources


Recommended open learning materials



DARTH Decision Modeling Course: Free online course in health decision modeling


ISPOR Modeling Guidelines: Modeling practices and HTA methodology


Decision Modelling in Health Economic Evaluation (book): Key textbook for HTA decision modeling

title: About R-HTA in LMICs
text:
R-HTA is a consortium focused on promoting the use of R for health economics and health technology assessment (HTA). While its core members are from the Americas and the UK—representing government institutions like NICE, academia, and industry—R-HTA aims to serve a global community of analysts.



To empower LMIC (low- and middle-income countries) health systems with cutting-edge, reproducible, and accessible analytical tools for HTA using the R language.
Learn more about our workshops and learning resources designed to build skills for both beginners and experienced practitioners.




The R-HTA in LMICs chapter is the regional arm of the R-HTA consortium. Its mission is to: - Introduce R as a viable alternative to costly software - Support LMIC analysts and institutions in adopting R for HTA - Share tailored educational resources and workshops
We believe that open-source tools like R can help bridge the digital and economic divide in health analysis, fostering a community that thrives on collaboration and shared innovation.




HTA in LMICs is often constrained by: - Heavy reliance on proprietary software like Microsoft Excel and TreeAge - Limited budgets and access to advanced tools - Lack of hands-on, practical R training for HTA analysts
Meanwhile, health systems in LMICs are rapidly evolving, especially under the drive toward Universal Health Coverage (UHC). This creates a growing need for: - Complex modelling (e.g. oncology, rare diseases) - Scalable and reproducible tools - Local capacity-building
Contact us to discuss how we can help bring these capabilities to your institution.





Workshops & Training Opportunities — covering R, Health Technology Assessment (HTA), essential R packages, and practical tips for applying R in diverse contexts. Explore more on our Workshops page.
Advanced tutorials — building cost-effectiveness models and interactive apps using Shiny.
Free resources and code — all available on our GitHub.

We work side-by-side with partners, offering mentoring, technical assistance, and bespoke solutions for regional health needs.




Scalable
R handles complex HTA models more efficiently than spreadsheet-based tools. Read more
Reproducible
With Git, RMarkdown, and Shiny, models can be: - Shared with version control - Easily updated - Delivered as interactive tools
Open and Community-Driven
R’s vibrant health economics ecosystem includes: - BCEA - heemod - hesim - dampack




We welcome collaborators and learners alike—join us in building a stronger foundation for HTA in LMICs using R.
Visit our contact page to connect with our team.
